symptoms.symptom_name: Multiple chronic (6-month) hyperkeratotic, rough, fleshy-greyish papules on the dorsal fingers, previously painless

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Chronic, multiple, rough hyperkeratotic papules on the fingers of a child are the hallmark of common viral warts.
Molluscum papules are smooth, pearly and umbilicated, not rough and verrucous.

symptoms.symptom_name: Acute traumatic partial avulsion of the largest lesion with brisk continuous bleeding disproportionate to a graze

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Warts have a rich superficial vascular core and bleed briskly when the keratotic roof is avulsed — the mechanism of this presentation.
Brisk bleeding from a small skin lesion is also the hallmark of a pyogenic granuloma — the important-not-to-miss benign vascular mimic that cannot be excluded remotely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Thrombosed capillaries ('black dots') at the base and disruption of the normal dermatoglyphic pattern across the lesion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Thrombosed capillaries seen as black dots and loss of the skin-line pattern crossing the lesion are the classic diagnostic features of a viral wart.
Molluscum lacks black dots and a disrupted dermatoglyphic pattern, showing a central umbilication instead.

symptoms.symptom_name: Several similar smaller lesions on the adjacent finger with a history of picking/scratching (autoinoculation)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Multiple lesions spreading by autoinoculation from scratching is typical of viral warts.
Pyogenic granulomas are characteristically solitary; multiplicity argues against it and towards viral warts.

symptoms.symptom_name: Afebrile and systemically well, with no surrounding cellulitic erythema, warmth, swelling, purulence or ascending red streaks

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Secondary bacterial infection of the traumatised lesion
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of fever and local infective signs makes an established secondary infection unlikely now, though the broken skin remains a mandatory safety-net target.

symptoms.symptom_name: Haemodynamically well child (alert, normal colour, not floppy) with modest pressure-responsive blood loss and no bleeding-diathesis history

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A well child with modest, pressure-responsive bleeding and no bleeding tendency confirms a benign controllable mechanical bleed rather than haemorrhagic compromise or a coagulopathy; drives haemostasis coaching over emergency escalation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Clinician-measured vitals and weight (HR 95, BP 105/65, Temp 36.8, RR 18, SpO2 99%, Wt 28 kg) and in-person haemostasis with silver-nitrate chemical cautery and a protective dressing

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Sealed in-person measurement/procedure: normal paediatric vitals confirm haemodynamic stability, and definitive haemostasis was achieved by chemical cautery — a physically-present clinician action the AI Doctor cannot perform and cannot measure without a device.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person review of the finger/lesion for definitive haemostasis if firm pressure fails and to confirm the benign nature of the lesion
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The assessment telehealth cannot do — direct inspection/dermoscopy to confirm a wart versus a pyogenic granuloma, and chemical cautery or a pressure dressing if bleeding is not controlled by first aid.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Routine GP/pharmacist review for definitive wart treatment of the remaining lesions (topical salicylic acid or cryotherapy) once the acute lesion has healed
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Definitive treatment of the background warts is elective and, in a child, best selected and dosed in person rather than over telehealth.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Skin biopsy / histology of the lesion
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not indicated — the appearance is classic for benign viral warts in a child; reserve only for an atypical, non-healing or diagnostically uncertain lesion on in-person review.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Coagulation screen / full blood count for a bleeding disorder
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not indicated now — the bleeding is mechanical, from a vascular wart core, slows with pressure, and there is no personal or family bleeding-tendency history. Consider only if bleeding is truly disproportionate/recurrent or other bleeding/bruising emerges.
